30:0.rendering: The words of King Lemuel, the oracle his mother taught him.
30:0.note: This verse introduces a rare mother's oracle to her royal son, echoing the Bible's pattern of wisdom passed down like a covenant inheritance. It sets up the famous poem on the woman of noble character, a portrait of covenant faithfulness that reflects God's own wise rule over creation and ultimately points forward to the wise and virtuous king the whole story has been longing for.

30:1.rendering: What are you doing, my son? What are you doing, son of my womb? What are you doing, son of my vows?
30:1.note: This opening line from King Lemuel's mother uses a Hebrew wordplay on "mah" (what) and "bar" (son) to form a rhythmic, urgent chant, like a mother calling her child back from the edge. It echoes the Bible's larger pattern of wisdom passed from parent to child, from the covenant faithfulness of Israel's mothers and fathers all the way back to the garden, inviting the next generation to choose the path of life over folly.

30:2.rendering: Do not give your strength to women, or your ways to those who destroy kings.
30:2.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for a warrior's might or a king's vigor, tying back to the royal instructions in Proverbs 31:1-9; it's a warning against squandering covenant faithfulness and royal image-bearing through alliances that lead to ruin, a pattern echoing Israel's kings from Solomon onward and pointing forward to the true King who stewards his strength perfectly.

30:3.rendering: It is not for kings, Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, or for rulers to crave strong drink.
30:3.note: This verse echoes the ancient warning given to Israel's kings in Deuteronomy 17, where excessive wine signals a ruler losing self-control and forgetting the law, a pattern that repeatedly led Israel's leaders into covenant failure and exile.

30:4.rendering: Otherwise, they will drink and forget what is decreed, and twist the rights of all the oppressed.
30:4.note: This warning about a king whose mind is clouded by wine echoes the Bible's deep pattern of rulers who lose their ability to do justice, from Pharaoh to the kings of Israel. It sets up the ideal of the wise ruler who instead uses his strength to speak up for the vulnerable, a thread that finds its fulfillment in Jesus, the true king who remembers the oppressed and enacts justice for them.

30:5.rendering: Give strong drink to the one who is perishing, and wine to the bitter in soul.
30:5.note: This proverb sits inside a royal instruction from a mother to her king son, inviting him to use his power to care for the vulnerable rather than indulge himself. The Hebrew word for "perishing" echoes the language of those on the edge of death or exile throughout the story, a theme that finds its ultimate expression when the true King gives himself as the remedy for humanity's deepest bitterness and ruin.

30:6.rendering: Let him drink and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.
30:6.note: This verse sits inside a king's wise instruction about the dangers and proper use of strong drink, given to a ruler who must maintain clear judgment for the vulnerable. The Hebrew word for "misery" here echoes the "affliction" of Israel's slaves in Egypt, inviting us to see how the Bible's story wrestles with the tension between earthly relief from suffering and the deeper rescue God is bringing through his anointed king.

30:7.rendering: Open your mouth for the voiceless, for the cause of all who are passing away.
30:7.note: This verse sits at the heart of the ancient king's instruction to his son, calling him to use royal power to speak up for those with no voice, especially the vulnerable who are fading from society. It echoes the Bible's larger pattern of God as the ultimate advocate for the oppressed, a thread that runs from the Exodus deliverance through the prophets and finds its climax in Jesus, the true King who opens his mouth for the voiceless.

30:8.rendering: Speak up and render justice for the voiceless, for the cause of all who are perishing.
30:8.note: The Hebrew word for "justice" here is mishpat, the same term used throughout the Bible for the kind of right-ordering that reflects God's own character, seen in the exodus deliverance and the covenant commands to protect the vulnerable. This call to use one's voice and power for the voiceless echoes the pattern of God's kingdom, where the strong defend the weak, a thread that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the king who speaks up for the oppressed and gives his life for the perishing.

30:9.rendering: A woman of strength, who can find? Her worth is far beyond jewels.
30:9.note: The Hebrew word here for "strength" is the same one used for a warrior's valor and God's own power. This closing portrait of Proverbs paints a picture of a woman who embodies the book's ideal of wisdom in flesh and blood, partnering with God to bring order, provision, and life to her world, a living image of the wisdom that builds the house.

30:10.rendering: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he will have no lack of gain.
30:10.note: The Hebrew word for "heart" here (leb) carries the full weight of a person's inner trust and confidence, the same word used when Israel is called to love God with all their heart. This capable woman becomes a living picture of covenant faithfulness, the kind of reliable partnership that echoes God's own trustworthy character and points forward to the ultimate bride whose husband, Jesus, finds his heart fully at rest in her.

30:11.rendering: She brings him good, and not evil, all the days of her life.
30:11.note: The Hebrew word for "good" here is tov, the same word God repeatedly uses in Genesis 1 to declare creation "good," and the word that echoes through the wisdom literature as the outcome of living in tune with God's design for the world. This woman isn't just a helpful spouse, she's an image-of-God partner who actively participates in making her household a little pocket of tov, of flourishing order amid the chaos, which is one of the Bible's core visions of what redeemed human life looks like.

30:12.rendering: She seeks out wool and flax, and works with eager hands.
30:12.note: The Hebrew word for "seeks" (d-r-sh) is the same one used for searching out wisdom or even seeking God himself, portraying this woman as an active explorer who brings raw creation materials into order through skillful work. This image echoes the Genesis 1-2 pattern of taking chaos and forming it into something good and life-giving, a thread that runs through the Bible's portrait of humans as image-bearing partners with the Creator, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus who takes the raw materials of a broken world and weaves them into new creation.

30:13.rendering: She is like the ships of merchants, she brings her food from far away.
30:13.note: The Hebrew word for "brings" here is the same one used for how God "brings" Israel out of Egypt and later "brings" them back from exile, painting this capable woman as an active agent who reaches beyond her immediate world to provide for her household, echoing the Bible's larger theme of faithful image-bearers who partner with God's own work of bringing abundance and order from distant places.

30:14.rendering: She rises while it is still night, and gives food to her household, and a portion to her servant girls.
30:14.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" here is the same one used for the daily bread or "provision" that God gives his people in the wilderness (Exodus 16) and for the "allotment" of land in the promised inheritance. This wise woman of strength mirrors the faithful provider God, who rises early to sustain his household with good things, a theme that echoes throughout the story from creation's provision to Jesus as the bread of life.

30:15.rendering: She considers a field and buys it, from the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.
30:15.note: This image of the capable woman echoes the wisdom of Genesis 1 where humans are called to rule and subdue the earth as God's image, turning potential into fruitful abundance. Her shrewd vision for land and vineyard connects to the Bible's larger pattern of covenant faithfulness producing life and provision, a thread that runs from Eden's garden to the fruitful vineyard of God's people and ultimately to Jesus as the true vine.

30:16.rendering: She wraps strength around her waist, and she makes her arms strong.
30:16.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for God's power in battle and for the fearless trust of the righteous, a quality that echoes the image-of-God vocation given to humans in Genesis 1. In the larger story, this capable woman becomes a portrait of covenant faithfulness that brings order and blessing to her world, a thread that later weaves into the New Testament's picture of the church as a strong, active partner in God's renewing work.

30:17.rendering: She senses that her trading is good, and her lamp does not go out at night.
30:17.note: The Hebrew word for "senses" here is ta'am, the same word for "taste" (as in, she tastes that her merchandise is good), painting a picture of the woman of strength intuitively discerning value like a connoisseur. This verse weaves into the larger biblical theme of wisdom as a skillful, all-night labor that brings light into darkness, echoing how God's wise image-bearers partner in creating order from chaos and how that same wisdom ultimately takes on flesh in Jesus, the light that never goes out.

30:18.rendering: She stretches out her hands toward the distaff, and her palms grasp the spindle.
30:18.note: This image of the woman of noble character working with distaff and spindle captures the ancient art of spinning thread, a daily rhythm of turning raw wool or flax into something strong and useful. It echoes the Bible's larger theme of humans as image-of-God partners with the Creator, taking the raw materials of creation and, through skillful, faithful work, bringing order, beauty, and provision out of chaos, a pattern that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus who weaves humanity and divinity together into a new creation.

30:19.rendering: She extends her hand to the poor, and she reaches out her palms to the needy.
30:19.note: The word for "extends" here is the same Hebrew verb used when God stretches out his hand to deliver or provide, painting this capable woman as an image-bearer who actively bridges heaven's abundance with earth's need. This small act of open-handed generosity echoes the Bible's larger pattern of covenant faithfulness, where God's people participate in his work of restoring shalom to the vulnerable, a thread that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus, who stretched out his hands for the poor and needy.

30:20.rendering: She is not afraid of the snow for her household, for all her household is clothed in scarlet.
30:20.note: The word for "scarlet" here is the same Hebrew term used for the costly, vibrant coverings of the wilderness tabernacle, linking this wise woman's provision for her family to the sacred space where heaven and earth overlapped. In the larger biblical story, this image of fearless abundance echoes God's covenant faithfulness, providing protective covering for his people even in harsh seasons, a thread that reaches its climax when Jesus becomes the ultimate provision and shelter for God's household.

30:21.rendering: She makes coverings for herself, her clothing is fine linen and purple.
30:21.note: The Hebrew word for "coverings" here is the same one used for the tabernacle curtains and the high priest's garments, linking this capable woman to the place where heaven and earth overlap in beauty and order. Her choice of expensive purple dye, a symbol of royalty in the ancient world, hints at the royal image-of-God vocation that runs from Genesis 1 all the way to the restored humanity we see in Jesus.

30:22.rendering: Her husband is known in the city gates, where he sits among the elders of the land.
30:22.note: The image of the husband sitting at the city gates, among the elders, taps into an ancient pattern where the gate was the place of wisdom, justice, and community leadership. This verse shows how the capable woman's household order creates space for her husband to embody the wise image-of-God role in the public square, a thread that runs from the garden ideal through Israel's covenant life and finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the wise king who brings order to God's people.

30:23.rendering: She makes linen garments and sells them, and supplies belts to the merchants of Canaan.
30:23.note: The capable woman in Proverbs 31 isn't just industrious at home. She's a shrewd businesswoman whose hands create value that flows outward, trading with the merchants of Canaan, the very land that once symbolized Israel's greatest temptation and failure. This image of productive, outward-focused work echoes the original mandate to the image of God, to fill the earth and cultivate blessing that reaches the nations, a thread that finds its ultimate fulfillment in the royal bride, the new Jerusalem, whose light and goods draw all peoples in Revelation 21.

30:24.rendering: She is clothed with strength and dignity, and she laughs at the days to come.
30:24.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same one used for the valor of warriors and the power of God throughout the Bible, portraying this woman as a heroic image-bearer who embodies covenant faithfulness. This vision of dignity and fearless confidence in the future echoes the Bible's larger story of God's people being clothed with divine strength to face exile, chaos, and the unknown, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus who clothes his people with his own resilient life.

30:25.rendering: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue.
30:25.note: The Hebrew word for "teaching" here is torah, the same word used for God's own instruction that shapes his people. This wise woman doesn't just speak cleverly; she embodies covenant kindness (hesed), the loyal love that holds relationships together. In the Bible's larger story, she echoes the voice of Woman Wisdom from earlier in Proverbs, pointing forward to how Jesus would perfectly weave wisdom and hesed into a life that renews the world.

30:26.rendering: She keeps watch over the goings-on of her household, and the bread of idleness she does not eat.
30:26.note: The Hebrew word for "keeps watch" (tsophiyah) is the same one used for a lookout or spy, portraying this woman as an alert guardian over her household's rhythms and resources, much like the wise person in Proverbs who stays attentive to the way of wisdom instead of drifting into folly. This image of vigilant stewardship echoes the Bible's larger theme of humans as image-bearing caretakers of God's good world, a role that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, the faithful overseer of God's household.

30:27.rendering: Her children rise up and call her happy, her husband also, and he praises her.
30:27.note: The Hebrew word for "happy" here is the same one used throughout the Bible for the state of blessing and flourishing that comes from living in tune with God's wisdom, like the "blessed" person in Psalm 1 or the beatitudes of Jesus. In the grand story of Proverbs, this isn't just domestic praise. It's the fitting conclusion to a book that begins with Lady Wisdom calling people to a life of order and delight, showing how that wisdom, embodied in a person, creates a household that echoes the harmony of Eden and anticipates the new creation community where heaven and earth fully overlap.

30:28.rendering: Many daughters have done noble things, but you surpass them all.
30:28.note: The Hebrew word for "noble things" here is the same one used throughout Proverbs to describe the wise, capable life of those who fear the Lord, a life that embodies the image-of-God vocation to rule and create order. In the bigger story this closing praise of the woman who fears Yahweh points forward to the ultimate faithful one whose wisdom and covenant love bring flourishing to his people, Jesus.

30:29.rendering: Charm is deceptive, and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD, she is to be praised.
30:29.note: The word for "fears" here is the same Hebrew term used throughout Proverbs for the beginning of wisdom, a deep awe and reverence before the Creator that reorients all of life. This verse brings the book's long exploration of two paths (wisdom or folly, Lady Wisdom or the strange woman) to a surprising climax in a real person who embodies covenant faithfulness, echoing the image-of-God theme where true human glory is found not in self-exaltation but in loyal relationship with Yahweh, a thread that runs all the way to Jesus as the ultimate wise and faithful one.

30:30.rendering: Give her the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the city gates.
30:30.note: The poem closes by calling the community to publicly honor this woman's productive life, echoing how wisdom literature often pictures the "fear of the Lord" bearing tangible fruit that blesses the world. It's a fitting capstone to Proverbs, where the personified Woman Wisdom builds her house and prepares her table; here she steps into real life as an image-bearer whose faithful work brings heaven-and-earth harmony right into the everyday rhythms of family and society.
